coredata.dc:description: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) and gallium (Ga) are widely used in many fields and their excessive presences will pose serious threats to the environment and life health. Therefore, developing an intelligent detection method for rapidly tracking and determination of CIP and Ga3⁺ concentration in environments and organisms is of great significance. In this work, a europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) with unique structure and performances was prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Eu-CDs can efficiently overcome the problem of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in complex environments to track CIP and Ga3⁺ with highly sensitivity. The "antenna effect" of non-radiative energy transfer is introduced to explain the pathway on fluorescence transition of Eu-CDs through DFT theoretical calculations and mechanism investigations, which provide a theoretical basis for the design and development of novel carbon dots with specific functions. Eu-CDs can successfully be applied to fluorescence imaging in organisms, moreover, it also exhibits excellent application potentials in the field of anti-tumor and antibacterial. In addition, a portable intelligent detection platform based on Eu-CDs-hydrogel device have been developed, which provides a new strategy for rapidly detection of CIP and Ga3⁺ in real samples.

head.ce:title: Intelligent sensing platform based on europium-doped carbon dots for dual-functional detection of ciprofloxacin/Ga3 + and its tracking in vivo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) and gallium (Ga) are widely used in many fields and their excessive presences will pose serious threats to the environment and life health. Therefore, developing an intelligent detection method for rapidly tracking and determination of CIP and Ga3⁺ concentration in environments and organisms is of great significance. In this work, a europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) with unique structure and performances was prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Eu-CDs can efficiently overcome the problem of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in complex environments to track CIP and Ga3⁺ with highly sensitivity. The "antenna effect" of non-radiative energy transfer is introduced to explain the pathway on fluorescence transition of Eu-CDs through DFT theoretical calculations and mechanism investigations, which provide a theoretical basis for the design and development of novel carbon dots with specific functions. Eu-CDs can successfully be applied to fluorescence imaging in organisms, moreover, it also exhibits excellent application potentials in the field of anti-tumor and antibacterial. In addition, a portable intelligent detection platform based on Eu-CDs-hydrogel device have been developed, which provides a new strategy for rapidly detection of CIP and Ga3⁺ in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP), as a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is extensively employed because of its excellent antibacterial properties, low toxicity, and good oral absorption [1,2]. However, the characteristic of slow metabolism and degradation in use makes it difficult to be rapidly metabolized in organisms to result in its accumulation for a long time in the environment [3,4]. Excessive CIP residue may cause allergic reactions in the body and bring about serious risks to human health such as central nervous system disorders, intestinal diseases, and so on [5–7]. Therefore, the World Health Organization (WHO) has formulated the maximum limit of CIP residue in food and environment. For example, the CIP residue limit in fish is stipulated no more than 100 μg/kg [8,9]. Gallium (Ga) is a metal element with broad applications in the fields of semiconductors and electronics [10–13]. In recent years, the potential application of gallium ions (Ga3+) in the field of biomedical had received increasing attention. In terms of tumor diagnosis, gallium can accurately locate the position and size of tumors through radionuclide imaging technology [14,15]. Gallium can act as a competitive inhibitor of Fe (III) in the body to destroy iron homeostasis at the lesion site and interfere with its metabolic process, thereby achieving the effects on the inhibition of tumors and bacteria growth [16,17]. However, the imaging tracking and potential impact on the dosage of gallium also need to be carefully evaluated in the biomedical field [18]. Furthermore, the antibacterial potential and synergistic effect between Ga3+ and CIP are worthy of in-depth exploration. The simultaneous detection of Ga3+ and CIP can provide crucial support for relevant applications such as bio-imaging, disease diagnosis, and environmental monitoring. Thus, it is practical significance for exploring an efficient method to quickly track and determine the concentration of CIP and Ga3+ in various environments and organisms.

Current mainstream technologies for qualitative and quantitative analysis of CIP and Ga3+ include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [19], spectrophotometry [20], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [21], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELI) [22], and mass spectrometry [23], etc. However, high cost, harsh operation requirements, and lack of portability for these methods restrict their practical applications [24–27]. carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial with low toxicity, simple synthetic process, excellent luminescent properties and biocompatibility, shows excellent application prospects in fluorescence bio-imaging and the tracking and determination of organic molecules and metal ions [18,28–31]. Therefore, the fluorescence analytic methods based on CDs have become a highly potential candidate strategies [32]. At present, there are only fewer reports for the detection of CIP and Ga3+ base on CDs [33–39]. For example, Yin et al. based on silver-doped carbon dot successfully realized the quantitative analysis of CIP [40]. Kim et al. designed a composite material composed of rhodamine and CDs to achieve selectively detection of homologous elements of Ga3+ through energy transfer sensing mechanism [41]. Liu group described a nitrogen-doped fluorescent CDs for investigation of gallium from environment [38]. But there are no literatures for dual functional determination of CIP and Ga3+ based on CDs so far. Lanthanides coped CDs with favorable properties including adjustable chemical structure, long fluorescence lifetime and photosensitizing ligand have been used to detect and identify multiple substances [33,34,37,42–46]. At present, most of reported CDs were synthesized through the transformation of existing materials and lack of directional surface modification [47–50]. Moreover, the mechanisms on recognition and detection are vague and lack of theoretical support [45,51]. CDs are easily aggregated to cause fluorescence quenching at higher concentrations, it is still a challenge to develop a novel CDs for sensing CIP and Ga3+ with portable application in real samples.

Europium-doped CDs was widespread applied to tracking and imaging in organisms and regional recognition of gallium in the fields of anti-tumor and antibacterial due to its excellent fluorescence emission, monomer dispersibility and extreme environmental stability. For example, Ameen et al. synthesized a combining europium-based ligand CDs and MOF material to realize ratio fluorescence detection of Ag+/cysteine and cell imaging [52]. Based on organic clay and Ln elements raw materials, Chen et al. reported a multifunctional fluorescent CDs probe with specific functions for detection of pyridine dicarboxylic acid and Cu²+ [53]. Wu et al. developed a lanthanide coordination CDs material for high-sensitivity detection of benzaldehyde and Fe3+ [54]. In this work, dialdehyde starch from corn starch and triethanolamine used as the precursor, a europium-doped CDs (Eu-CDs) was successfully prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Eu-CDs presents highly fluorescence emission from bright red to blue color and can conduct cascade tracking of CIP and Ga3+ with a high-contrast effects. The coordination mechanism on between Eu-CDs and CIP was investigated by DFT theoretical calculations. In addition, the applications for Eu-CDs in biomedicine was also investigated, and a hydrogel detection system had been designed and established, which provided a novel idea for intelligent and portable detection of CIP and Ga3+ on-site.

The corn starch and triethanolamine in the experiments were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., and europium chloride and ciprofloxacin were purchased from Shanghai Haohong Biomedical Technology Co., Ltd. Other reagents were chemical experiment standards without further treatment.

The structure and morphology of Eu-CDs were characterized via JEOL2010 transmission electron microscope (TEM) from Yongke Optical Instrument Co., Ltd. (China) and a Phenom ProX (Netherlands) field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM). The element distribution was analyzed by an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) of Phenom ProX (Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. (USA) was used to analyze the surface chemical state of Eu-CDs. The fluorescence spectra were obtained at F-7000 of Baihe Instrument Technology Co., Ltd. (China). The FTIR-650S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer from Tianjin Gangdong provided the FT-IR spectra. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra and pH values were respectively collected by ATU-1810DPC spectrometer and a PHS-3C.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, 0.5 g corn starch was fully mixed with 0.6 mol/L sodium periodate solution and stirred at 40 °C and dark environment for 4 h to provide the oxidized starch (dialdehyde starch, DAS). Subsequently, 0.35 g DAS, 0.7 g triethanolamine and 0.25 g europium chloride were uniformly mixed, and transferred to a high-pressure autoclave with polytetrafluoroethylene bladder to heat to 200 °C for 5 h via a typical hydrothermal synthetic process. After completion, the solution was filtered through a vacuum filter with a pore size of 0.22 μM, then the filtrate was dialyzed in ultrapure water for 30 h through a 500 Da dialysis bag to obtain Eu-CDs solution. The optimal synthetic process for Eu-CDs are the feeding ratio of 1:2:1, 200 °C and 5 h at pH = 4.0 (Table S1 and Table S2 for details). The obtained solution of Eu-CDs was freeze-dried to form the Eu-CDs solid for subsequent experiments.

The prepared Eu-CDs solid was dispersed in deionized water to form a 250 mg/L Eu-CDs solution. The Eu-CDs solution was added to a pH = 7.5 of phosphate buffer solution, then different concentrations of CIP standard solutions were added to the Eu-CDs solution. Sequentially, the fluorescence emission spectra of each group of solutions were measured at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The emission intensities of the Eu-CDs+CIP system at 591 nm, 612 nm, and 702 nm were used to establish a detection standard curve for quantitative analysis of CIP. Potential interfering substances (ions, amino acids, similar drug molecules) were added to the system to carry out the anti-interference experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Eu-CDs+CIP probe for detecting Ga³⁺
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared 250 mg/L of Eu-CDs solution was uniformly mixed with a 200 μmol/L CIP solution to generate the Eu-CDs+CIP solution with red fluorescence, which were added to different concentrations of Ga³⁺ solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded under 360 nm excitation wavelength for quantitative analysis of Ga³⁺. To investigate the anti-interference ability of Eu-CDs+CIP to Ga³⁺ detection, an equal amount of Ga³⁺ and the other metal cations solution were added to the Eu-CDs+CIP solution, and the fluorescence emission intensities at 612 nm were recorded as an interference control at 360 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties and DFT calculation of Eu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The redox performance of Eu-CDs was further studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV, EA161 eDAQ). In this studies, a platinum sheet, platinum wire and Ag/Ag⁺ were respectively used as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode. The electrolyte solution is composed of 0.10 M (Bu)₄NBF₄ and freshly dried dimethylformamide (DMF) [55].

To comprehensively explore the binding interaction energy among Eu-CDs, CIP, and Ga³⁺, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 software [56]. Ground state simulation calculations, geometric optimization and frequency calculations were employed by the methods of the B3LYP functional and 6–311G (d,p) basis set. To improve the calculation accuracy and consider the influence of dispersion effects on the long-range potential, an empirical dispersion correction method B3LYP-D3 was established. The system serves water as the solvent, and the integral equation form - polarizable continuum model (IEF-PCM) was used to look into the solvation effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological application and construction of intelligent detection CIP/Ga³⁺ system
ce:section.ce:para: Medaka fish (from the growth process of fish eggs to juvenile fish) was used as a sample to monitor the whole-processing and fluorescence imaging performance of Eu-CDs to CIP/Ga³⁺ in organisms. The newly born medaka fish eggs were cultured in a 200 μM CIP solution at 28 °C for 24 h. Then, fish eggs were washed and placed in a 250 mg/L of Eu-CDs solution for 5 min, and the fluorescence changes were observed under a fluorescence microscope (360 nm fluorescence emission wavelength). After 20 min, transferred to a 30 μM Ga³⁺ of solution for 5 min and again observed the fluorescence changes [57]. 250 mg/L of Eu-CDs solution was used to culture in the growth process from fish eggs to juvenile fish (7 days) to inspect the biological toxicities through the changes in the vital signs of the fish. Considering of triethanolamine and europium may be pollution sources of Eu-CDs, and high-performance liquid chromatography was used to examine their residual concentration. The results confirmed that the contents of triethanolamine and europium in the Eu-CDs solution were far lower than the limit standards in GB31604.27–2016.

In the intelligent detection system, a hydrogel chip was prepared using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as the raw material. Firstly, the solutions containing Eu-CDs and Eu-CDs+CIP were respectively prepared. Subsequently, they were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to prepare hydrogel precursors. Then, the repeated freeze - thaw method was employed to shape the hydrogel into 2-millimeter-thick slices. Finally, the encapsulation was performed to fabricate a hydrogel chip detection card. Under a 360 nm ultraviolet analyzer, after dropping the tested substances of each gradient concentration onto the chip, the standard curve of fluorescence changes for each concentration and the corresponding RGB values are obtained. The obtained RGB values of each gradient concentration are compiled into a mobile phone APP program written in Java language. The smartphone camera is called to locate and photograph toward the hydrogel chip detection card. The APP program analyzes the RGB value of the chip and forms the corresponding concentration of detected target substance.

The morphological characteristics of Eu-CDs in both dispersed and aggregated solid states can be obtained by TEM and SEM. It can observe from the TEM image Fig. 1 b, the synthesized Eu-CDs is spherical and presents monomer dispersibility and size uniformity in solution. The average diameter of Eu-CDs is 3.2 nm, mainly distributes in a narrow range from 2.0–4.2 nm (Fig. 1 c). Eu-CDs with a lattice size of 0.176 nm is similar with the (102) diffraction plane of graphite (sp2) carbon, and the single nanosphere structure is clearly shown in Fig. 1 b. The crystallinity of dialdehyde starch and Eu-CDs is investigated by XRD as depicted in Fig. S1. The characteristic peaks of dialdehyde starch has scattering at 2θ values of 15.6°, 17.4°, and 24.1°. In contrast, multiple peaks disappears, and only a broad peak appears at 2θ = 19.5° in the XRD pattern of Eu-CDs. These indicate that both the reflection intensity and the peak sharpness have decreased, thereby confirming the reduction in the crystallinity of Eu-CDs to indicate that the Eu-CDs has higher purity. The lattice size of Eu-CDs calculated according to the Bragg's law is similar with the result of the graphite-like diffraction plane lattice obtained by TEM. Different sizes of CDs may due to different numbers of functional groups at the boundary of CDs to result in differences in carbon layer spacing. As shown in Fig. 1 d, Eu-CDs presents good aggregation state from the SEM image of CDs solid. In view of the characteristic of uniform distribution of each element shown in the EDS images (Fig. 1 e- 1 h), triethanolamine and europium are demonstrated to participate in the structural construction of Eu-CDs.

The structure and composition of Eu-CDs were studied by FT-IR spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 1 i. The characteristic peak of the stretching vibration of the aldehyde group (-CO bond) at 1730 cm⁻¹ in dialdehyde starch (DAS) gradually disappears during the generation of Eu-CDs. In contrast, a new characteristic peak of -CN at 1620 cm⁻¹ is introduced in the FT-IR spectrum of Eu-CDs. In addition, the wide peak from 2500–3500 cm⁻¹ and weak peaks of -OH and -NH at 3461 cm⁻¹ in Eu-CDs indicate that DAS reacts with triethanolamine to produce a carbon-nitrogen bond, and the -OH group is oxidized to form carboxyl group [57]. The characteristic peaks of -OEu and -NEu at 667 cm⁻¹ and 613 cm⁻¹ can prove the generation of europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs)[58]. The surface state of Eu-CDs is further investigated by XPS as shown in Fig. S2, Eu-CDs is mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and europium, and their ratios are 60.36 %, 4.43 %, 31.77 % and 3.44 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of Eu-CDs in Fig. 1 j can further explain that the unique peak of the -C-C/-CC bond of Csp2 at 284.94 eV and forms the graphite-like structure of Eu-CDs. The Csp2 hybrid orbital is bonded, and the carbon atom presents a planar triangular geometry. The -CN/-CO bond at 288.25 eV and the -C-O bond at 286.31 eV indicate that the successful introduction of triethanolamine during the formation of Eu-CDs to produce the functional groups with oxygen-rich characteristics such as hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups [59]. The characteristic peaks of -CO (529.88 eV), -OH (531.36 eV) and -OEu (530.33 eV) in the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1 k) can also confirm the contribution of these oxygen-rich groups to the complexation with Eu3+. The high-resolution spectrum of Eu 3d obtained from Eu-CDs appears the peaks at 1164.78, 1155.68, 1143.48 and 1135.08 eV (Fig. S3), which align with the bonding between oxygen atoms and europium. The valence state of Eu is mainly Eu³ ⁺ (3d³/₂ and 3d⁵/₂). The -C-N (398.28 eV) and -CN (399.78 eV) in the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1 l) and the -NEu characteristic peak at 401.18 eV, are all attributed to the introduction of nitrogen from triethanolamine to Eu-CDs [49,60]. These results further confirm a successful synthesis of Eu-CDs via the hydrothermal process.

The unique structural system of Eu-CDs with rich oxygen groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl) and amino groups greatly enhances its compatibility and durability. The doping of europium can combine with the functional groups on the surface of CDs to reduce the interaction among CDs and aggregation, and enhancing its stability in aqueous solution. After four months of storage in an unclosed environment at room temperature, no deposited or suspended nanoparticles were observed in the Eu-CDs solution. After being irradiated by a 360 nm ultraviolet lamp for 5 h, it still maintains good recognition performance and there is no obvious photobleaching loss to be observed. These characteristics indicate that Eu-CDs may have good application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Eu-CDs for detection and testing of CIP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-CDs, Eu-CDs+CIP and Eu3⁺+DAS+TEA+CIP were measured under the optimized excitation wavelength of 360 nm. No fluorescence emission was observed in the Eu-CDs solution, but the mixed solution of Eu-CDs and CIP exhibited obvious bright red fluorescence and presented four characteristic peaks (5D₀→7F₁ (591 nm), 5D₀→7F₂ (612 nm), 5D₀→7F₃ (654 nm) and 5D₀→7F₄ (702 nm)) [61,62]. Although the mixed solution of Eu3+, DAS, TEA and CIP also appeared similar fluorescence emission, the fluorescence was quite weak. The ultraviolet absorption spectrum demonstrated that the Eu-CDs combined with CIP to form a new complex Eu-CDs+CIP (Fig. 2 b). The prominent red fluorescence of Eu-CDs+CIP indicated that the energy transferred from CIP to Eu-CDs to make the f-orbital of Eu³⁺ to obtain energy in the complex [63].

At a 250 mg/L concentration of Eu-CDs solution, the influence factors such as pH, temperature and storage time in the environment were investigated and the results were shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs+CIP in common organic solvents such as MeOH, EtOH, DMSO revealed that Eu-CDs can efficiently monitor CIP in various solvents, and the fluorescence changes were more obvious in higher polar solvents (Fig. S5). Eu-CDs demonstrated excellent fluorescent response to CIP in 3.0–10.0 pH range, but the red fluorescence intensely weakened when pH value reached 11.0. Obviously, the pH ranges were enough wide to cover most of practical application and biological environments. Fig. 2 c showed the fluorescence response and stability of Eu-CDs at different environmental temperatures for storage 1–30 days, and the fluorescence intensity respectively decayed 0.8 %, 1.2 %, and 1.6 % at 338 K, 318 K, and 298 K after 30 days. The data demonstrated that temperature and storage time had less influences on the performances of Eu-CDs+CIP.

The investigation for Eu-CDs to quantitative detection of CIP, as shown in Fig. 2 d, the Eu-CDs solution (250 mg/L) at pH = 6.0 exhibited obvious gradient fluorescence emission 591 nm, 612 nm and 702 nm after adding 5.0–215.0 μM of CIP, and the fluorescence intensity significantly increases with the enhancement of the concentration of CIP. It can observe from Fig. 2 e that the fluorescence changes trend of Eu-CDs solution corresponds to the changes of CIP concentration. Fig. 2 f shows the photoluminescence behavior of Eu-CDs' fluorescence response to different concentrations of CIP under 360 nm ultraviolet lamp. When the CIP concentration is lower than 190 μM, there is a linear relationship between the titration concentration of CIP and the fluorescence intensity at 591 nm, 612 nm and 702 nm (Fig. 2 g- 2 i). The correlation coefficients (R²) of three linear fittings are 0.9963, 0.9966 and 0.9973, respectively, and the displayed trends in fluorescence changes are basically the same. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of CIP are calculated to be 0.014 μM and 0.042 μM, respectively, which are lower than recent reported CIP sensor at the concentration level (Table S3). When 215 μM of CIP is added to the N-CQDs solution, red fluorescence of the solution quickly produces and reaches the maximum value within 0.5 s, then tends to be stable after 2.5 s (Fig. S6). The reaction conforms to the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (correlation coefficient R² = 0.9971, rate constant K = 0.3319). Using quinine sulfate as the standard, the relative quantum yield of the Eu-CDs+CIP was obtained to be 38.95 % (parameters are shown in Table S4) by the following equation:

where φ stands for the fluorescence quantum yield, F refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity, and A is the absorption value. The subscript “m” and “n” correspond to the reference and the sample.

To study the selectivity and anti-interference of Eu-CDs as a multipurpose sensor for detecting CIP, the effects of various ions and possible interfering molecules in CIP detection were tested. As shown in Fig. S7, the presences of various anions and cations have no influence on Eu-CDs to detection of CIP. The fluorescence intensity (Fig. S8) and absorbance of Eu-CDs+CIP (Fig. S9) are not affected by the other coexistence of ions. In biological applications, similar drug molecules and common amino acids may interfere the detection results. As Fig. 2 j and 2 k shown, various drug molecules (Fig. 2 l) and common amino acids (Fig. 2 m) have no significant interference on detection of CIP. Compared the combination of Eu-CDs with different quinolone antibiotics, only the fluorescence spectrum of CIP exhibits obvious changes, which can attribute to the specific interaction between Eu-CDs and the β-diketo acid configuration sites of CIP. However, the other quinolone antibiotics have significant differences in spatial conformation and electronic effects when binding with Eu-CDs. Therefore, these molecules have no significant impacts on the fluorescence of Eu-CDs. The above data confirmed that Eu-CDs had highly selectivity and strong anti-interference potential for CIP detection in actual samples, indicating that it may be a better sensor for CIP detection.

CIP with β-diketo acid structure has strong complexing ability and can form a stable coordination intermediate with metal ions [2], and CIP also appears strong complexing effect on Ga³⁺. Inspired by the synthesis of diketo complexed with metal gallium as anticancer drugs, Eu-CDs+CIP was expected to achieve ratiometric fluorescence probe for sensing of Ga³⁺. To investigate the relevant performances of Eu-CDs+CIP in fluorescence intensity and sensitivity, the concentration optimization of Eu-CDs and CIP solutions was carried out, and 250 mg/L Eu-CDs and 200 μM CIP were determined as the optimal concentration ratio for the subsequent researches. Select Ga³⁺ and common metal ions (Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Zn²⁺, Co²⁺, Mn²⁺, Hg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Ni²⁺, Cu²⁺, Ba²⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Cr³⁺, Al³⁺) to investigate the selectivity for Eu-CDs+CIP to detection of Ga³⁺. To prevent the interference of excessive CIP, a Eu-CDs+CIP sensing system including 250 mg/L of Eu-CDs and 200 μM of CIP is configured. Fig. 3 a is the selectivity test for Eu-CDs+CIP to various metal ions, and the results show that only when Ga³⁺ presents, the red fluorescence of the Eu-CDs+CIP system is destroyed and the fluorescence emission wavelength blue shifts to 450 nm. Moreover, the coexistence of various metal ions has no obvious effects on Eu-CDs+CIP to Ga³⁺ detection (Fig. 3 c). Considering the influence factors such as pH, temperature and storage time to Eu-CDs+CIP on the detection of Ga³⁺. Fig. 3 b exhibits the fluorescence response of Eu-CDs+CIP to Ga³⁺ at different temperatures and storage times. The fluorescence intensities of Eu-CDs+CIP+Ga³⁺ stored at 338 K, 318 K, and 298 K for 30 days respectively decays 0.18 %, 0.21 %, and 0.28 %. The results indicate that temperature and storage times have less effect on the action of Eu-CDs+CIP to Ga³⁺ response. As shown in Fig. 3 e, the fluorescence response of Eu-CDs+CIP to Ga³⁺ is satisfactory at pH ranges from 3.0 to 10.0, but blue fluorescence greatly weakened after pH value is more than 10.0. The possible reason is that excessive OH- deposition affects the detection of Ga³⁺. When the concentration of Ga³⁺ buffer solution is lower than 30 μM and pH < 3.0, the stability of the Eu-CDs+CIP sensing system for detecting Ga³⁺ will be significantly affected due to the influence of competitive protonation and other effects (Fig. S10). The above results demonstrate that the Eu-CDs+CIP system had specific selectivity, stability and anti-interference ability to Ga³⁺ under the concentration range from 0.1–75.0 μM (Fig. 3 d). As Ga³⁺ was gradually added, the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-CDs+CIP system at 591 nm, 612 nm and 702 nm slowly decreased, and the characteristic peak gradually increased and blue shifts to 450 nm. When Ga³⁺ was added to the Eu-CDs+CIP solution, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum value within 0.3 s and tended to stabilize after 2 s (Fig. 3 f). It can conclude that the reaction complies with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (correlation coefficient R² = 0.9958, rate constant K = 0.2821)[64]. As shown in Fig. 3 g, there is a linear relationship between the added concentration of Ga³⁺ and the changes of fluorescence intensity at 450 nm, and the correlation coefficient (R²) of linear fitting is 0.9975. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of Ga³⁺ are 0.022 μM and 0.066 μM, respectively, which are lower than the reported Ga³⁺ sensor at the concentration level (Table S5). As the optical diagram Fig. 3 h under 360 nm ultraviolet lamp shown, it can obviously observe that bright red fluorescence of Eu-CDs+CIP transfers to blue as the addition of Ga³⁺, and its quantum yield is 36.98 % (parameters are shown in Table S4), and the binding model of Eu-CDs+CIP with Ga³ is shown in Fig. 3 i. Therefore, the Eu-CDs+CIP system can serve as a ratio fluorescent probe for monitoring and quantitative detection of Ga³⁺ in various environments.

By measuring the cyclic voltammetry curve of Eu-CDs in solution state and combining with DFT theoretical calculation, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV, FT-IR and XPS, the changes of Eu-CDs after the sequential addition of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and Ga³⁺ are thoroughly investigated. In the subsequent experimental data analysis part, the detection mechanism of Eu-CDs for CIP and Ga³⁺ is elaborated in detail. The β-diketoacid configuration of CIP, as a bidentate ligand, is conducive to Eu³⁺ on Eu-CDs coordination with Ga³⁺. By analyzing the fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs, CIP, and Eu-CDs+CIP (Fig. S11), it can obtain that Eu³⁺ will coordinate with water molecules when Eu-CDs is in solution state, which dues to high-frequency -O-H oscillation to cause non-radiative quenching of Eu³⁺ in Eu-CDs [65]. As the addition of CIP with a unique diketoacid structure, it easily combines with Eu-CDs [31]. When CIP is irradiated by the excitation light at 360 nm, it can transfer the initial energy to the Eu-CDs system through the "antenna effect" under the synergistic effect of CDs [66]. At the same time, the fluorescence emission characteristic of Eu³⁺ (5D₀→7F₁, 5D₀→7F₂, 5D₀→7F₄) is prominent. The antenna effect in the energy absorption and transfer processes is based on the antenna functions can absorb light of specific wavelengths through the intramolecular energy transfer process (electron transfer). The absorbed energy is transferred to the europium ions (fluorescence center of Eu-CDs), causing the excited-state energy level distribution of the fluorescence center to change, and further affecting the characteristics such as the fluorescence emission intensity and wavelength. Due to the multi-functional groups and spherical characteristics of Eu-CDs, a relatively large number of Eu³⁺ can be coordinated. Furthermore, multiple CIP molecules can be attracted in the solution and an enrichment effect can be achieved, making Eu-CDs+CIP present red fluorescence with high quantum yield [67].

As the ultraviolet spectra of Eu-CDs, CIP, Eu-CDs+CIP, and Eu-CDs+CIP+Ga³⁺ Fig. 4 a shown, compared with CIP, the high-intensity peak of Eu-CDs+CIP at 278 nm is blue-shift to 272 nm, and the two consecutive peaks at 315–330 nm are red-shift to 325–340 nm. These data indicate that there is a coordination interaction between Eu-CDs and CIP, resulting in energy transfer from CIP to Eu³⁺, further confirming the "antenna effect" [68,69]. In addition, after adding Ga³⁺ to Eu-CDs+CIP, the continuous peak intensity at 325–340 nm decreases, and the high-intensity peak at 272 nm is red-shift to 284 nm. These results demonstrate that the addition of Ga³⁺ gradually replaces Eu-CDs and coordinates with CIP to form a new coordination bond. To confirm this speculation, based on the modified Benesi-Hildebrand equation, the complexation constants of CIP with Eu-CDs, Ga³⁺ and common metal ions at the same temperature are calculated:

0 is the fluorescence intensity of CIP, ΔF is the fluorescence change after adding Eu-CDs and Ga³⁺ to the CIP suspension, K is the binding constant, and n is the complexation ratio.

The calculated KCIP-Ga and KCIP-Eu-CDs are 1.38 × 10⁻⁹ and 6.30 × 10⁻⁸, respectively, and indicates that Ga³⁺ has a higher coordination ability with CIP molecules than Eu-CDs, confirming the inference due to the failure of the "antenna effect", the red fluorescence of Eu-CDs+CIP gradually disappears and the blue fluorescence of the Eu-CDs+CIP+Ga³⁺ system gradually increases [2,70]. In addition, by calculating the binding constants of CIP with interfering metal ions (Table S6), it can prove that the binding ability of CIP to Ga³⁺ is better than that of interfering metal ions such as Al³⁺, Ca²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cu²⁺ and Pb²⁺. These studies provide a basis for the anti-interference abilities of Eu-CDs+CIP in identifying and detecting Ga³⁺.

To further explore the binding mode of Eu-CDs with CIP and Ga³⁺ in sequence, FT-IR and XPS analyses were performed. FT-IR analysis is shown in Fig. 4 b, the addition of CIP causes the -O-Eu bond in Eu-CDs to shift from 665 cm⁻¹ to 695 cm⁻¹. After the subsequent addition of Ga³⁺, the stretching vibration peak of the -O-Ga bond at 620 cm⁻¹ appears, and the peak of -C-O weakens and the peak area of -C-N increases. All these phenomena indicate that the addition of CIP and Ga³⁺ will change the coordination in the system. XPS analysis of the system before and after the detection of CIP and Ga³⁺ shows that there is an obvious Ga³⁺ element peak on the surface of the Eu-CDs+CIP+Ga³⁺ system (Fig. S12), and the content is 4.08 % (Fig. S13). In Fig. 4 c, the contents of -C-O and -CN/-CO bond in the C1s spectrum decrease from 46.61 % and 13.84 % to 19.21 % and 8.76 %, respectively. During the detection process, the content of -C-C/-CC bond increases from 39.55 % to 72.03 %. This may be because the sequential addition of CIP molecules and Ga³⁺ destroys the oxygen structure of the original functional groups and forms a new complex structure. It is worth noting that the content of the -OC bond in the O1s spectrum decreases from 38.76 % to 12.85 % (Fig. 4 d), which further proves the change of the original functional group structure caused by a new formed complex to generate the coordination change of -Eu-O and -Ga-O [71]. In the N1s spectrum Fig. 4 e, an obvious -N-Ga bond peak appears at 406.57 eV, which means that the N element in Eu-CDs+CIP also produces a corresponding interaction during the detection of Ga³⁺. Therefore, since Eu-CDs forms a new complex with CIP through most of the oxygen-rich groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) and a small part of amino groups on the surface through Eu³⁺ further combination with Ga³⁺ to reconstruct the structure of the complex. According to the theory that the change of molecular crystal structure during the photoluminescence process leads to the change of the electron migration pathway in the system [60], A conclusion of the fluorescence shift effect caused by a non-radiative energy transfer mechanism is proposed.

To confirm the existence of the electron transfer mechanism. First, the cyclic voltammetry curve of Eu-CDs is determined (Fig. 4 f), and the reduction potential Ered of Eu-CDs is obtained as 0.476 eV. Then, the optical band gap Eg = 4.221 eV is obtained from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum of Eu-CDs (Fig. 4 g). The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of Eu-CDs are estimated by using empirical formulas:

The LUMO and HOMO energy levels of Eu-CDs are calculated to be −4.876 eV and −9.097 eV, respectively. Using the B3LYP functional calculation method in Dmol3 mode, the ELUMO and EHOMO energy levels of CIP are obtained as −3.255 eV and −5.314 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 h, after CIP is added, a complex (Eu-CDs+CIP) is formed between Eu-CDs and CIP. After being irradiated by 360 nm ultraviolet light, the excited state electrons of Eu-CDs transfer to the CIP orbital. The electrons in the radiative form (FL emission) of Eu-CDs+CIP migrate back to the HOMO energy level of Eu-CDs. This confirms the generation of red fluorescence of Eu-CDs+CIP caused by the "antenna effect". After Ga³⁺ is added to the Eu-CDs+CIP system, due to the stronger coordination ability of CIP and Ga³⁺, the β-diketoacid structure of CIP performs six-membered double coordination with Ga³⁺ and forms a complex aggregation system (Eu-CDs+CIP+Ga³⁺) with Eu-CDs. The energy level can calculate to be ELUMO= −4.563 eV, EHOMO= −9.901 eV, and Eg= 5.338 eV after CIP is coordinated with Ga³⁺. It can know that the excited electrons of CIP + Ga³⁺ cannot migrate between Eu-CDs, resulting in the gradual disappearance of red fluorescence in the system [72]. Compared with Eu-CDs+CIP, because the band gap of Eu-CDs+CIP+Ga³⁺ (Eg = 5.338 eV) is greater than the band gap of Eu-CDs of 4.221 eV, the fluorescence emission will be blue-shift to Em = 450 nm under the same excitation conditions. According to the above results, the conjecture of the non-radiative energy transfer mechanism is confirmed, providing a theoretical basis for Eu-CDs to detect CIP and Ga³ ⁺.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological applications of Eu-CDs and Eu-CDs+CIP
ce:section.ce:para: CIP is a broad-spectrum antibacterial drug, and Ga³⁺ has important performances in anticancer and antibacterial aspects. Therefore, it is crucial for Eu-CDs to track and detect CIP and Ga³⁺ in real biological conditions. Firstly, medaka fish eggs and juvenile fish were continuously cultured in a 250 mg/L of Eu-CDs solution for 7 days, and their changes in life status and physiological metabolism were continuously observed and recorded. Next, the biological toxicity of Eu-CDs was judged via the standard biological observation experiment. The experimental results demonstrated that the survival rate of medaka fish eggs and larvae was 100 %. During the continuous culturing process for 7 days, through the observation of their metabolic activities and other life characteristics, all relevant data were found to be within the normal range, further indicating Eu-CDs with low physiological toxicity.

The fluorescence microscope was used to explore the perception ability of Eu-CDs for CIP and Ga³⁺ in fish eggs and juvenile fish. As can see from Fig. 5 a, the fish eggs cultured in the Eu-CDs solution have no any special fluorescence changes under natural and ultraviolet light. But the fish eggs cultured in the CIP solution for 24 h were placed in the Eu-CDs solution for 5 min, there is no change under natural light, but it can observe that bright red fluorescence generates inside and outside under ultraviolet light. After 20 min, they are transferred to the Ga³⁺ solution for 5 min, then observed red fluorescence of the fish eggs gradually weakens and is replaced by blue fluorescence, which was clearly seen by enlarged local image. Employing the above method to test juvenile medaka fish, it can find that the effect of CIP mainly makes the internal organ area of the fish produce bright red fluorescence. The subsequent addition of Ga³⁺ will gradually weaken the red fluorescence to turn into blue fluorescence (Fig. 5 b). Continuous detection of the fluorescence intensity of fish eggs with red fluorescence for 30 min can find that the fluorescence attenuates to only 18 % (Fig. 5 c). when the concentration changes of CIP from 10.0 to 40.0 μM and Ga³+ from 5.0 to 20.0 μM, the corresponding red fluorescence intensity for fish eggs increases 60 % and decreases 98.3 % (Fig. 5 d and 5 e). These results indicate that Eu-CDs has excellent durability and concentration perception ability when tracking CIP and Ga³⁺ in organisms.

Ga³⁺ as a competitive inhibitor of Fe(III) in the body can destroy the iron homeostasis at the lesion site and achieve antitumor or antibacterial effects. The studies on the Eu-CDs+CIP system for monitoring gallium in transferrin was shown in Fig. 5 f. When Eu-CDs+CIP is added to transferrin in presence of or absence of Ga³⁺ after 5 s, it can see from Fig. 5 g that transferrin respectively shows red or blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. In the step-by-step enlarged effect diagram of Ga-transferrin detection (Fig. 5 h), it can observe that the Eu-CDs+CIP to recognition of Ga³⁺ can be used at the protein level. Therefore, Eu-CDs+CIP may have great application potential in regional tracking of Ga³⁺ in the field of antitumor and antibacterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of intelligent sensing system for recognition of CIP and Ga³⁺
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the limitations of the solution system in portable ratio fluorescence detection [73]. To further expand the application of Eu-CDs in CIP and Ga³⁺ detection, a portable sensing device based on hydrogel-encapsulated Eu-CDs was developed. The fluorescence signal of the device was recognized by a smartphone to perform quantitative analysis of analyte. The optical stability of Eu-CDs in various environments and the elastic three-dimensional lattice structure of PVA hydrogel ( Fig. 6 a) provided the space for doping Eu-CDs to distribute in the hydrogel network space. As shown in Fig. 6 b, by preparing a highly hydrophilic and light-transmitting Eu-CDs hydrogel into a thin sheet and installing it on an opaque hydrophobic sheet, a sensing chip can obtain.

As shown in Fig. 6 c, the sensing chip is encapsulated in a detection card. By dropping the analyte into the detection hole below the detection card, the above display window can show the fluorescence changes under 360 nm ultraviolet light. The fluorescence color changes within the positioning point range of the detection card in the dark box is photographed by a smartphone, and the analytic results are obtained by combining the independently developed smartphone recognition app (the core code parameters of the app are shown in Fig. S14). The effect diagram of Eu-CDs hydrogel chip on CIP and Ga³⁺ detection is shown in Fig. 6 d. Under 360 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiation, the chip with different concentrations of CIP gradually transitions from no special fluorescent color to bright red. In the tested range from 5.0 – 200.0 μM, the color CIE-R value of the chip is linearly related to the concentration of CIP (Fig. 6 e). The addition of Ga³⁺ range from 0.5 – 70.0 μM to the detection chip after adding 215 μM of CIP, and the fluorescence color of the chip gradually changes from bright red to bright blue. The analysis shows that the natural logarithm of the ratio of the CIE B value to the CIE R value of the chip color at this time (Ln(B/R)) is exponentially related to the Ga³⁺ concentration (Fig. 6 f). The two sets of chip color CIE values and distributions are shown in Fig. 6 g. During the detection process, after the smartphone recognizes the chromaticity RGB value of the sensing chip, the concentration range of the detected object can be calculated according to the above data.

In this work, a novel europium-doped carbon dot (Eu-CDs) was successfully prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal process employing dialdehyde starch, triethanolamine and EuCl3 as the substrates. Eu-CDs as a ratio fluorescence probe can specifically recognize CIP and Ga³⁺ with highly sensitivity and visual color changes. During the recognition process, the introduction of CIP stimulates Eu-CDs to generate specific bright red fluorescence by the means of the "antenna effect", and the detection limit is low to 0.014 μM. With the addition of Ga³⁺, the fluorescence in the coordination system of Eu-CDs with CIP gradually shifts to bright blue with the detection limit of 0.022 μM. The step-by-step detection performance for Eu-CDs to CIP and Ga³⁺ is better than most of reported results. Through DFT theoretical calculations and mechanism investigations, an "antenna effect" plus non-radiative energy transfer mechanism is innovatively proposed to explain the fluorescence transformation caused by the change of the CDs coordination structure. The innovative studies not only provide an analytical detection tool, but also successfully applies to biological tracking, imaging and regional recognition in the fields of antitumor and antibacterial. In addition, an intelligent and portable platform based on Eu-CDs hydrogel detection chip have been constructed, which provides a new pathway for rapidly determination of CIP and Ga3+ in real sample.

CIP is a widely used antibiotic that can persist in the environment, inhibit the growth of microorganisms, cause changes in microbial community structure, and promote the emergence of resistance genes. High concentrations of ciprofloxacin pose a risk to ecosystems. Gallium is widely used in the electronics industry. Gallium poses risks to aquatic ecosystems and long-term exposure to gallium can affect human health. The presence of CIP and gallium in the environment at the same time may interact with each other to aggravate the adverse impact on the ecosystem, so it is necessary to further study their environmental thresholds.
